fuzzy_prd: We need a shared in-memory name registry and address resolver for a host that runs many isolated virtual networks, each containing multiple workloads that must discover one another by friendly name rather than raw address. A configuration loader reads a simple text-based description for each virtual network — the network's gateway listen points and the membership list of workloads attached to it, including their addresses and the aliases they should be reachable by — and builds lookup tables once at startup. The library must then support six query types: report the listen addresses of all networks broken down by address family; translate a workload name into its addresses from the perspective of a requesting caller; translate a raw address back into the names that own it; retrieve the custom upstream resolvers declared for a specific workload; retrieve the shared upstream resolvers inherited by all workloads on a given network; and report which network or networks a given address belongs to. Name matching must be case-insensitive so that mixed-case registrations do not cause misses. When a name lookup finds no match, the system must signal a non-existent domain rather than returning empty success. A single malformed network description must be silently skipped — it must not abort processing of the remaining well-formed networks. The core domain logic must be cleanly separated from any command-dispatch or input-parsing layer, and the dispatch layer reads a structured operation request and routes it through the internal resolution engine.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: pub fn parse_configs(dir: &str, filter_search_domain: &str) -> AardvarkResult<(DNSBackend, HashMap<String, Vec<Ipv4Addr>>, HashMap<String, Vec<Ipv6Addr>>)>

trigger_keywords: unknown name not found unregistered name lookup
name does not exist missing name query
what happens when name cannot be resolved
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the queried name is not registered on any network the requester belongs to, the output must be exactly the single token 'nxdomain' followed by a newline: 'nxdomain\n'. An empty address list is NOT acceptable — the sentinel value 'nxdomain' must be emitted instead.

trigger_keywords: container has no upstream dns servers declared none
per-container dns server field empty missing not set
what is output when container dns servers absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a container line has no fifth field (no per-container dns-servers declared), querying that container's address with op='container_dns_servers' must output exactly 'dns_servers=none\n'. The literal sentinel value is 'none' (lowercase). Do not output an empty list or omit the line.

trigger_keywords: bind addresses output ordering sort networks
network name order bind_addresses op result
how are multiple networks ordered in bind_addresses output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
For op='bind_addresses', networks are enumerated in lexicographic (alphabetical) sort order by network name. For each network the output is exactly three lines: 'network=<name>
', 'bind_v4=<comma-separated IPv4 addresses or empty>
', 'bind_v6=<comma-separated IPv6 addresses or empty>
'. Address families are listed preserving the order they appear in the first line of the config file.

trigger_keywords: case insensitive name matching uppercase lowercase mixed case
name registered with capital letters queried lower
case sensitivity of container name resolution
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Name lookup is fully case-insensitive. Names are stored normalized to lowercase internally. A query for 'HELLOWORLD', 'helloworld', or 'HelloWorld' all resolve to the same address list. The lookup entry is lowercased before matching against the stored keys.

trigger_keywords: malformed bad config network skipped parse error
invalid network configuration file tolerated
what happens when one network config is malformed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A single malformed network configuration (e.g., an unparseable address token on the first line, or a container line with fewer than 4 space-separated fields) causes that network to be silently skipped. The overall parse does NOT fail — well-formed networks continue to be processed and returned. A config containing only malformed networks yields empty output (no error, no networks reported).

trigger_keywords: dual stack IPv4 IPv6 both addresses returned
container has both v4 and v6 lookup result
multi-address resolution order IPv4 IPv6 combined
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a container has both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses, a forward lookup returns all addresses in registration order as a single comma-separated list on one 'addresses=' line. The order is: IPv4 addresses listed first (in the order they appear in field 2 of the container line), then IPv6 addresses (from field 3 of the container line). For example: 'addresses=10.89.0.2,fdfd:733b:dc3:220b::2\n'.

trigger_keywords: reverse lookup result format names registration order
address to names output comma separated
reverse_lookup op what is output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For op='reverse_lookup', the output is a single line 'names=<comma-separated names>\n' where names are in registration order (primary name first, then aliases in the order they were listed in the config). All names are returned lowercased. If the target address is not found, output is 'nxdomain\n'.

trigger_keywords: network_membership output format container address networks
ip_mappings which networks does container belong to
network membership deduplicated container in multiple networks
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For op='network_membership', the output is 'networks=<comma-separated network names>\n'. The network list is deduplicated — if the same container id appears in multiple networks, each network name appears at most once. Both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses of the same container resolve to the same network membership list. If the container address is not found, output is 'not_found\n'.

trigger_keywords: network level resolvers inherited by all containers
network scoped dns servers first line second token
network_resolvers op output format resolvers field
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: For op='network_resolvers', the network-level upstream resolvers are declared as the optional second space-separated token on the first line of the config file, with individual addresses comma-separated (e.g., '10.88.0.1 127.0.0.1,::2'). The output is 'resolvers=<comma-separated>\n'. All containers on that network inherit this list. If the container is not found, output is 'not_found\n'.

trigger_keywords: container line format fields space separated id ipv4 ipv6 names
config file format per container entry parsing
how many fields does a container line have and what are they
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each container line has exactly 4 required space-separated fields and 1 optional fifth field: '<container-id> <ipv4-list> <ipv6-list> <names> [<dns-servers>]'. The ipv4-list, ipv6-list, names, and dns-servers fields are each comma-separated. An address field may be empty (no address of that family). A line with fewer than 4 fields causes the whole network to be skipped as malformed.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

are there public test cases

what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The DNSBackend struct in src/backend/mod.rs, with its lookup(), reverse_lookup(), and get_network_scoped_resolvers() methods

context_pointers.vague_reference: a configuration loader reads a simple text-based description for each virtual network
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The parse_configs() function in src/config/mod.rs, which reads all files from a directory, each filename being the network name and the file content being the text config format

context_pointers.vague_reference: the dispatch layer reads a structured operation request and routes it through the internal resolution engine
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The rcb_tests/dispatcher/src/main.rs execution adapter which reads JSON from stdin, materializes config files into a temp directory, calls parse_configs(), dispatches on the 'op' field, and prints results to stdout

context_pointers.vague_reference: report the listen addresses of all networks broken down by address family
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The bind_addresses op, which uses the listen_v4 and listen_v6 HashMaps returned by parse_configs(), printing network=<name>, bind_v4=<...>, bind_v6=<...> lines sorted by network name via BTreeSet

context_pointers.vague_reference: signal a non-existent domain rather than returning empty success
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The backend.lookup() returning None triggers printing the literal string 'nxdomain\n' in the dispatcher; similarly reverse_lookup returning None prints 'nxdomain\n'

oracle_data.fallback_response: This project implements an in-memory DNS name registry and resolver for virtual container networks. The core public API is parse_configs(dir, filter_search_domain) which returns a DNSBackend plus two HashMaps for IPv4/IPv6 bind addresses. The DNSBackend supports lookup (forward), reverse_lookup, get_network_scoped_resolvers, and direct field access for ctr_dns_server and ip_mappings. The text config format has the network bind addresses on the first line and one container per subsequent line with fields: id, ipv4-list, ipv6-list, names[, dns-servers]. Name matching is case-insensitive. Unresolved names return 'nxdomain'. Containers without dns-servers declared return 'dns_servers=none'. Malformed network configs are silently skipped.

feature1_parse_bind_addresses.description: Parse a set of textual network-configuration files and report, per network, the gateway/bind addresses split into IPv4 and IPv6 families. Each file represents one network whose name is the file name; the file's first line lists the network's bind addresses (comma-separated, optionally followed by network-level upstream servers). The parser groups bind addresses by family preserving their listed order, and tolerates a malformed file by skipping that network entirely without failing the whole parse.

feature2_1_resolve_name.description: Resolve a name to its address list using the perspective of a requesting container IP. The requester's network membership is derived from configuration; the queried name may be the requester's own name, another container's primary name, or one of its aliases. The result is the ordered list of addresses registered for that name on the requester's network.

feature2_2_case_insensitive.description: Resolve a name case-insensitively. Names registered with mixed case are matched regardless of the case used in the query, so the same address list is returned whether the query is lower-case or upper-case.

feature2_3_unknown_name.description: Resolve a name that is not registered on the requester's network. When no address is registered for the queried name, the resolver reports a non-existent-domain outcome rather than an empty success.

feature2_4_dual_stack.description: Resolve a dual-stack name to all of its addresses. A name may carry both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses (and possibly several of each); resolving it returns every registered address in listed order. The requester may itself be reached over either family and still observes the full address list. A container may also be addressed by its identifier in addition to its name.

feature3_reverse_lookup.description: Given a requesting container IP and a target IP on the same network, return the registered name(s) and alias(es) for that target address, in registration order. Works for both IPv4 and IPv6 targets.

feature4_container_dns_servers.description: Report the per-container upstream DNS servers declared in configuration. A container line may optionally end with a comma-separated list of upstream resolver addresses (IPv4 and/or IPv6); querying that container's IP returns that list. A container with no declared upstream resolvers is reported as having none.

feature5_network_scoped_resolvers.description: Report the network-level upstream DNS resolvers that apply to a container. When a network's first configuration line declares network-scoped resolver addresses, every container on that network inherits them; querying any such container's IP returns that inherited resolver list.

feature6_network_membership.description: Report which network(s) a container address belongs to. Each address registered in configuration is mapped to the set of networks the owning container participates in (deduplicated); querying an address returns that network list. Both IPv4 and IPv6 addresses of the same container resolve to the same membership.
